Name: Talisman: Advanced
Description: Workflow / approval ontology with cross-entity lookups and multi-step derivation.

Workflows.summary_rich: The **projects pinned to the corkboard**. Each workflow is a labelled container for an ordered chain of `WorkflowSteps`; the chain length rolls up into `CountOfSteps` and trips `HasMoreThan1Step` once a second step lands. Most rows here are single-step today — *Asset Management* is the one with two, and is the live example of the aggregation firing.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Short machine-friendly name for the workflow. Used for programmatic reference and URL slug generation.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable title of the workflow. Maps to dct:title from Dublin Core. Example: 'Production Deployment Workflow', 'Employee Onboarding'.

schema.name: Description
schema.Description: Detailed description of the workflow's purpose and scope. Maps to dct:description from Dublin Core. Should explain what business goal the workflow achieves.

schema.name: Identifier
schema.Description: External system identifier for cross-referencing. Maps to dct:identifier from Dublin Core. This is the join key back to document management systems, ticket systems, or other operational systems.

schema.name: Modified
schema.Description: Last modification timestamp. Maps to dct:modified from Dublin Core. Critical for answering CQ5: 'Which workflows haven't been reviewed or updated in twelve months?'

schema.name: WorkflowSteps
schema.Description: Reference to workflow steps. Represents the ntwf:hasStep relationship linking workflows to their constituent steps.

schema.name: CountOfSteps
schema.Description: Calculated count of workflow steps in this workflow. Useful for workflow complexity analysis and reporting.

schema.name: HasMoreThan1Step
schema.explanation_rich: **A calculated field reading an aggregation.** `CountOfSteps` rolls up the step count via `COUNTIFS`; this field then derives a boolean from that count. Two layers of DAG, one definition each. Worked example: *Asset Management* currently has two `WorkflowSteps` (`assign-task-to-team`, `some-new-task`), so `CountOfSteps = 2` and this field is `TRUE`. Every other workflow has exactly one step, so it's `FALSE`. Insert a third step row for any workflow and that workflow's flag flips on the next read — the rulebook recomputes the chain end-to-end.

WorkflowSteps.summary_rich: Where the **cross-entity wiring** is most visible. Each step names an `AssignedRole`, and the rulebook then *reaches through* that role to its owning department (`AssignedRoleDepartment`) and branches on the result (`ExecutionActorType`) to decide whether the step is run by a human, an AI agent, or an automated pipeline. Change a step's assigned role and both derived fields recompute on the next read.

schema.name: Workflow
schema.Description: Foreign key to the parent workflow. Represents the inverse of ntwf:hasStep, enabling navigation from step to its containing workflow (ntwf:isStepOf).

schema.name: SequencePosition
schema.Description: Integer ordinal position of the step within its workflow. Maps to ntwf:sequencePosition, declared as owl:FunctionalProperty (each step has exactly one position). Enables positional queries.

schema.name: AssignedRole
schema.Description: Foreign key to the Role responsible for executing this step. Maps to ntwf:assignedRole. Critical for implementing Heuristic 2 (role-agent separation): steps point to roles, not directly to agents.

schema.name: RequiresHumanApproval
schema.Description: Boolean flag indicating whether a human agent must fill the assigned role. Maps to ntwf:requiresHumanApproval. Enables answering CQ3: 'Which steps require human decisions vs. AI execution?'

schema.name: ApprovalGate
schema.Description: Foreign key to ApprovalGate if this step is a decision checkpoint. When populated, indicates this step blocks workflow execution until explicit authorization is given.

schema.name: PrecededBySteps
schema.Description: Reference to steps that must complete before this step can execute. Part of the ntwf:precedesStep transitive ordering relationship.

schema.name: ExecutionActorType
schema.explanation_rich: **The nested-IF on a cross-entity lookup.** This is the field talisman-basic doesn't have. It reads `AssignedRoleDepartment` (itself a lookup through `Roles`) and runs it through a three-branch `IF` chain to classify the step's executor. Worked example: *manager-review* is assigned to the *editor* role, whose owning department is `finance` — none of the three IF branches match, so the field falls through to `BLANK()`. Reassign that step to a role owned by a `HumanAgent`-typed department and the same formula resolves to `"HumanAgent"` on the next read, in every substrate, with no application code in the loop.

schema.name: AssignedRoleDepartment
schema.explanation_rich: **Reach through one entity to read a field on the next.** The step doesn't know its department directly — it only knows its role. This lookup walks `WorkflowSteps.AssignedRole` → `Roles.OwnedBy` and surfaces the department on the step row. Worked example: *legal-compliance-check* is assigned to the *hr* role; the *hr* role's `OwnedBy` is `administration`, so this field reads `administration`. Reassign the step to *finance* and the same read returns `public-relations` — the department travels with the role assignment, without anybody writing a JOIN.

schema.name: WorkflowSteps
schema.Description: Back-reference to workflow steps that use this approval gate. Enables finding all steps requiring this specific gate.

schema.name: EscalationThresholdHours
schema.Description: Integer number of hours that may elapse on a pending gate before the ntwf:delegatesTo chain activates. Maps to ntwf:escalationThresholdHours. Domain applies only to ApprovalGate individuals.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Ordinal sequence number for the relationship. Used for sorting and display.

schema.name: WorkflowStep
schema.Description: Foreign key to the step that comes BEFORE. The source of the 'precedes' relationship.

schema.name: Label
schema.Description: Human-readable display name. Maps to rdfs:label. Per Heuristic 6: if you cannot write a clear label, you do not yet understand the concept well enough to model it.

schema.name: Comment
schema.Description: Detailed description of the role's responsibilities and scope. Maps to rdfs:comment. Should define what the role covers, what it excludes, and how it differs from adjacent roles.

schema.name: OwnedBy
schema.Description: Foreign key to the Department that owns this role. Maps to ntwf:ownedBy (owl:FunctionalProperty). Enables answering CQ7: 'Which workflows involve both Engineering and Legal?'

schema.name: DelegatesTo
schema.Description: Foreign key to the next Role in the escalation chain. Maps to ntwf:delegatesTo. Enables answering CQ6: 'What happens when the VP of Engineering is unavailable?'

schema.name: WorkflowSteps
schema.Description: Back-reference to workflow steps assigned to this role. Inverse of WorkflowSteps.AssignedRole.

data.Comment: Has full access to all system features and settings.

data.Name: editor

data.Comment: Can create, edit, and delete content but cannot change system settings.

data.Name: viewer

data.Comment: Can view content but cannot make any changes.

data.Name: moderator

data.Comment: Can submit new content for review but cannot publish directly.

data.Name: manager

data.Name: support

data.Name: analyst

data.Name: guest

data.Comment: Limited access for temporary users or external collaborators.

data.Comment: Creates and delivers training materials to users.

data.Name: owner
data.Label: System Owner
data.Comment: Ultimate authority over the system, can assign or revoke any role.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Human-readable display name of the department. Should match organizational terminology for stakeholder communication.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable display name of the department. Should match organizational terminology for stakeholder communication.

schema.name: DisplayName
schema.Description: Machine-friendly name for programmatic reference.

schema.name: Roles
schema.Description: Back-reference to roles owned by this department. Inverse of Roles.OwnedBy.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Full name of the person. Maps to foaf:name. Note: FOAF's name property is appropriate for persons, not for software systems (which use schema:name).

schema.name: Mbox
schema.Description: Email address of the person. Maps to foaf:mbox. Used for notifications and organizational directory integration.

schema.name: Roles
schema.Description: Back-reference to roles currently filled by this agent. Inverse of Roles.FilledBy_HumanAgent.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display name of the AI agent. Maps to schema:name (not foaf:name, which is for persons).

schema.name: ModelVersion
schema.Description: Version string of the AI model. Maps to ntwf:modelVersion. Makes AI-produced artifacts auditable at the version level. The domain declaration means this property applies only to AIAgent individuals.

schema.name: Roles
schema.Description: Back-reference to roles currently filled by this AI agent. Inverse of Roles.FilledBy_AIAgent.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display name of the pipeline. Maps to schema:name (appropriate for software systems, unlike foaf:name which is for persons).

schema.name: Roles
schema.Description: Back-reference to roles currently filled by this pipeline. Inverse of Roles.FilledBy_AutomatedPipeline.

SampleDataGovernance.Description: Governance rules for which sample-data rows belong in this rulebook. The Talisman ADVANCED demo is supposed to mirror the worked ABox in the source article — the Production Deployment Workflow at Special Solutions — not a generic catalog of every workflow type. Each row below records one keep/remove decision with a reason and source citation. Used as a checklist when curating seed data; rows here are NOT consumed by substrate code, only by the data-curation process. Originated from LEGACY_PLANs/PLAN-to-cleanup-ADVANCED-table.md (now relocated to this project as PLAN-to-cleanup-ADVANCED-table.md).

schema.name: TargetTable
schema.Description: Which rulebook table this rule applies to (Workflows, WorkflowSteps, Roles, etc.).

schema.name: Action
schema.Description: keep | remove | replace

schema.name: ItemName
schema.Description: The specific row name this rule covers (or 'ALL' when the rule applies to every current row in the target table).

schema.name: Reason
schema.Description: Why this item is kept/removed/replaced.

schema.name: SourceCitation
schema.Description: Where in the source article this decision is grounded — paragraph reference, count, or named entity.

schema.name: Confidence
schema.Description: explicit-in-article | implied-by-count | requires-full-NTWF-source — how directly the source supports the decision.

__meta__.Description: Project-level metadata that travels with the rulebook: tagline, motif, narrative descriptions, substrate list, signature rows, etc. One row per metadata key. Use ValueType to interpret StringValue vs JsonValue.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: The metadata key (e.g. 'tagline', 'motif_palette', 'substrates'). Unique within the table.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Identifier for this metadata entry. Mirrors MetaKey so the row is addressable by Name like every other table.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: How to interpret the value columns: 'string' (use StringValue), 'object' (parse JsonValue as JSON object), 'array' (parse JsonValue as JSON array).

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Plain string value. Populated when ValueType == 'string'; null otherwise.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value. Populated when ValueType == 'object' or 'array'; null when ValueType == 'string'.

data.Name: tagline
data.StringValue: Workflows, steps, roles — with IF logic, lookup chains, and a delegation ladder.

data.Name: description_rich
data.StringValue: Same workflow ontology as **talisman-basic**, pushed one rung up. Every workflow is still a chain of `WorkflowSteps`, each step still points at a `Role`, and the catalog of approvals still gates progress — but here the rulebook **reaches across entities** to compute new fields. `WorkflowSteps.AssignedRoleDepartment` hops Step → Role → Department in a single lookup; `WorkflowSteps.ExecutionActorType` then **branches on that result** with a nested `IF` to label each step as `HumanAgent`, `AIAgent`, or `AutomatedPipeline`. And the `Roles.DelegatesTo` chain encodes the escalation ladder declaratively — no procedural code, just a self-referential FK that every substrate walks the same way.

data.Name: use_cases
data.JsonValue: ["**Trace the delegation ladder.** Open a role (say *Manager*) and follow `DelegatesTo` backward through Contributor → Moderator → Viewer — the same chain that fires when an approval gate times out past its `EscalationThresholdHours`.", "**Reassign a step to a role in a different department.** Edit `WorkflowSteps.AssignedRole` and watch `AssignedRoleDepartment` re-derive through the lookup, then `ExecutionActorType` re-branch through the nested IF — two derived fields, one edit.", "**Ask which workflows span two departments.** Use the `AssignedRoleDepartment` lookup to group steps by owning department; *Contract Review* and *Asset Management* both touch more than one.", "**Promote a step into a multi-step workflow.** Add a row to `WorkflowSteps` pointing at an existing `Workflow`; `CountOfSteps` rolls up and `HasMoreThan1Step` flips to TRUE the same instant.", "**Compare to talisman-basic.** Open the basic rulebook side-by-side: same five table shapes, no cross-entity lookups, no IF, no delegation chain. The advanced demo is the **same skeleton with the muscle on**."]

data.Name: journal_seed
data.StringValue: Fifteen workflows pinned to the board, sixteen steps in flight. Asset Management is the only one with more than one step — watch the aggregation flip when you add the next.

data.Name: CMCC_Summary
data.StringValue: Airtable export with schema-first type mapping: Schemas, Data, Relationships (FK links), Lookups (INDEX/MATCH), Aggregations (SUMIFS/COUNTIFS/Rollups), and Calculated fields (formulas) in Excel dialect. Field types are determined from Airtable's schema metadata FIRST (no coercion); the build FAILS if metadata is unavailable rather than silently inferring from formula/data analysis.

data.Name: conversion_metadata
data.JsonValue: {"source_base_id": "appwN9EAp8IeIxM23", "table_count": 9, "tool_version": "2.0.0", "field_type_mapping": "checkbox→boolean, number→number/integer, multipleRecordLinks→relationship, multipleLookupValues→lookup, rollup→aggregation, count→aggregation, formula→calculated", "export_mode": "schema_first_type_mapping", "type_inference": {"enabled": true, "priority": "airtable_metadata (NO COERCION) → formula_analysis → reference_resolution → data_analysis (last-resort inference; build SHOULD FAIL rather than silently use this)", "airtable_type_mapping": "checkbox→boolean, singleLineText→string, multilineText→string, number→number/integer, datetime→datetime, singleSelect→string, email→string, url→string, phoneNumber→string", "data_coercion_hierarchy": "Only invoked when Airtable schema is unavailable (which SHOULD make the build fail). Coercion order: datetime → number → integer → boolean → base64 → json → string", "nullable_support": true, "error_value_handling": "#NUM!, #ERROR!, #N/A, #REF!, #DIV/0!, #VALUE!, #NAME? are treated as NULL"}}
